A $10K investment that day would be worth ~$485M today.

That is what happens when AI turns memory into a bottleneck across HBM, DRAM & storage while GPT-5.5, Claude Opus 4.7 & Gemini 3 Pro push toward bigger context, longer reasoning & persistent agent memory.

PEG > 2 starts to push into the danger zone

This is Peter Lynch’s favorite metric and I like it too because it ties valuation to growth. I prefer using a 2026 to 2028 window so the multiple adjusts for how fast the business is actually compounding.

For example $AAOI may trade at ~80x forward earnings but earnings are expected to grow from $0.88 in 2026 to $4.83 in 2028 implying a ~134% earnings CAGR and a PEG of ~0.6x.

I wrote this on April 26

Iran is running out of oil storage, and shutting down oil production is extremely damaging (true).

However, Iran has one more "card" to play: dump 2 million barrels/day into the Persian Gulf, creating an ecological disaster?

Timing Beats Explanation as Fractal Signals Turn Chaos Into Execution

Markets don’t pay you for explaining “why.”

They pay you for executing when.

WHY is interesting.
WHEN is alpha.

The market isn’t crazy.
It’s chaotic — and chaos has structure

This one was for grown-ups.

Maybe the aliens ARE real.
Maybe Iran IS the threat.
Maybe the Epstein files DO have nothing left.
But the trick isn't whether any single thing is true.
The trick is that you are being asked to look at one thing instead of all of them.

That has always been the bit.

A Department of War Production.
When the polls dip below 40, deploy a cryptid.

Three Americas tonight:

The Believers, staring up at the sky through binoculars.
The Doomscrollers, faces lit blue by the phone.
The Reader, sitting on a bench with a paperback.
None of them are talking to the others.

Lead-Lag Rule No. 1:

Markets do not care about aliens.
Markets care about who is distracted while the real allocation happens.
The signal is always in what they want you NOT to look at.

Watch the lagging indicators. They do not lie.

In a dim basement archive, a single thick folder labeled EPSTEIN UNREDACTED sits on a metal shelf, covered in dust.

A man walks past it carrying a flashlight.

His eyes are glued to a UFO video on his phone.

The hardest file to release is the one nobody wants you to want.

war.gov/UFO uses Comic Sans, an animated starfield, and a hit counter.

There is a guestbook link.

I have not seen a guestbook link since 2003.

Maximum transparency. Minimum CSS.
